title: Most AI agents are fake (here's a 10-second test)
summary: Jeff Su argues that most companies claiming to sell AI agents are not building genuine ones. He proposes a simple test: real generative AI and agentic products output novel generated content (text, images, video) or autonomous iterative thinking, while classification or numeric outputs do not constitute true agents or generative AI.
writeup: A Gartner report found that of thousands of companies claiming to build AI agents, only around 130 are building real ones. The video cuts through the marketing noise with a three-level framework: classification (Level 1), generative text (Level 2), and autonomous iterative action toward a goal (Level 3, the only true agent). Most products marketed as agentic are actually stuck at Level 1 or 2.

key_points: A Gartner report found roughly 130 companies out of thousands claiming to build AI agents are actually building real ones, making the term almost meaningless as used in most marketing.
Level 1 AI is simple classification (true/false outputs), exemplified by Gmail's spam filter, which is not even generative AI despite being called AI-enhanced.
Level 2 AI produces natural language text on demand, as in Google Docs' Help Me Write feature, making it generative but not agentic.

Level 3, the only genuine agent tier, involves autonomous iteration: Google's Antigravity accepts a design goal, writes code, and iterates until the output matches, without further human instruction at each step.
The practical test for whether a product is a real agent: does it act autonomously across multiple steps to reach a goal, or does it just classify or generate a single output?
